title: Earliest known dog genome pushes genetic record back 5,000 years
html_body:
What matters in science |
View this email in your browser Thursday 26 March 2026

Hello Nature readers,
We discover the earliest known dog genomes, investigate ‘grade inflation’ in PhDs and consider how environmental scientists can reinvent their research when funding is scarce.

If you enjoy this newsletter, please consider recommending it to a friend or colleague. Click here to forward it by e-mail. Thank you!
Dogs (Canis lupus familiaris) were domesticated from grey wolves (Canis lupus) at least 14,200 years ago, towards the end of the last ice age. (Martin Schroeder/CHROMORANGE via Alamy)

Dogs were early humans’ best friends

Researchers have identified the earliest known dog genomes, which push the genetic record for dogs back by more than 5,000 years. They recovered these genomes from remains of between 14,000 and 16,000 years old found at archaeological sites that span Europe and the Middle East. The team also identified an early domestic dog population (Canis lupus familiaris) that spanned Western Eurasia and was kept by diverse human hunter-gatherer groups. The findings show that dogs were exported and exchanged by various human groups, underlying dogs’ importance to early communities with different ways of living.

Nature | 6 min read
 Reference: Nature paper 1 & paper 2

Brain atlas maps chatter between regions

Scientists have created the first atlas of key patterns of ‘chatter’ between different areas of the brain over the entire human lifespan. Drawing on brain scans from 3,600 people — ranging from infants to centenarians — the guide maps a property called functional connectivity, which describes the level of coordination between separate brain regions. Such a guide could be useful for understanding when developmental issues and neurodegenerative conditions emerge, says neuroscientist Jakob Seidlitz. But it can’t capture how functional connectivity might differ between individuals.

Nature | 5 min read
 Reference: Nature paper
Go deeper with analysis from neuroscientists Richard Bethlehem and Daniel Margulies in Nature News & Views (7 min read)

A peer-reviewed AI scientist

‘AI Scientist’ — a system that aims to fully automate the scientific process — is one of the first artificial intelligence tools to go through the peer-review process at a leading academic journal. The resulting paper, published in Nature, updates a 2024 preprint that described the tool, including by toning down its reported capabilities. AI Scientist is a collection of ‘agents’ built on top of existing large language models, such as GPT-4o, that perform the full cycle of scientific discovery, from idea generation to testing its ideas to writing them up in a scientific paper.

Nature | 5 min read
 Reference: Nature paper

99%

The percentage of per- and poly-fluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), also known as forever chemicals, that conventional testing methods miss when used to analyse sediments in the Ganga river basin, India. (Nature India | 5 min read)
Reference: Environmental International paper

‘Grade inflation’ hits master’s and PhDs

The grades attained by master’s and PhD students at one large public university in the United States have crept up over the past two decades, without a demonstrable improvement in the quality of students’ work in that period. The spike likely amounts to what researchers call ‘grade inflation’, a phenomenon that has scarcely been documented at graduate level. The reasons behind such inflation aren’t clear, but university professors might be incentivized to give higher grades to receive better student evaluations or to boost enrolment, says psychologist and study co-author Vivien Lee.

Nature | 5 min read
 Reference: PLoS ONE paper
Many graduate programmes in the United States assign a grade point average, measured on a scale of 0 to 4. “What’s really striking is that we are seeing grade increases, even though 20 years ago grades were already high,” says Lee.

Features & opinion

Environmental scientists must refocus

With science funding dwindling in the United States, environmental scientists must find new and imaginative ways to continue their research, argues socio-ecologist Michael Paul Nelson. Scientists should take inspiration from humanities researchers and pursue projects that re-use existing data, or collaborate with other teams to pool resources when financial backing is scarce. “If we don’t find fresh ways of keeping science moving forwards, those bearing the cost will be the most vulnerable members of our community: graduate students, postdocs and early-career scholars, cast aside from languishing projects,” Nelson writes.

Nature | 5 min read

To the rescue of rockhoppers

In the Falkland Islands, effects of climate change such as changing weather patterns and warming oceans are threatening the population of southern rockhopper penguins (Eudyptes chrysocome). To help the birds adapt, researchers on the islands are finding innovative ways to protect the penguin population, including efforts to artificially bolster their nests, and reintroduce co-operative species such as imperial cormorants (Leucocarbo atriceps). These scientists might not be able to alter ocean conditions, “but to help them on land, at the breeding sites, is something we can actually do”, says seabird biologist Sarah Crofts.

bioGraphic | 12 min read

Quote of the day

“Meteorologists live in cities because that’s where the jobs are. They don’t live in small mountain towns. That’s what was missing.”

Meteorologist Bryan Allegretto combined his love of snowboarding and his “obsession with weather” to become the preeminent forecaster of ski conditions. (MIT Technology Review | 20 mn read)

Today I’m invested in an avian romantic drama. In the latest chapter, a pair of ospreys (Pandion haliaetus) — the catchily named female CJ7 and male 022 — have returned to a nest in Dorset, United Kingdom, that they’ve shared each mating season since 2022. The pair’s reunion signals that the drama of last year’s reunion — when CJ7 returned to their nest to find 022 with another female — was just a small sideplot in their love story.

Help us keep this newsletter faithful to your interests by sending your feedback to briefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Jacob Smith, associate editor, Nature Briefing
With contributions by Flora Graham

items.title: Dogs were early humans’ best friends
items.snippet: Researchers have identified the earliest known dog genomes, which push the genetic record for dogs back by more than 5,000 years. They recovered these genomes from remains of between 14,000 and 16,000 years old found at archaeological sites that span Europe and the Middle East. The team also identified an early domestic dog population ( Canis lupus familiaris ) that spanned Western Eurasia and was kept by diverse human hunter-gatherer groups. The findings show that dogs were exported and exchanged by various human groups , underlying dogs’ importance to early communities with different ways of living.

items.title: Brain atlas maps chatter between regions
items.snippet: Scientists have created the first atlas of key patterns of ‘chatter’ between different areas of the brain over the entire human lifespan. Drawing on brain scans from 3,600 people — ranging from infants to centenarians — the guide maps a property called functional connectivity, which describes the level of coordination between separate brain regions . Such a guide could be useful for understanding when developmental issues and neurodegenerative conditions emerge, says neuroscientist Jakob Seidlitz. But it can’t capture how functional connectivity might differ between individuals.

items.title: A peer-reviewed AI scientist
items.snippet: ‘AI Scientist’ — a system that aims to fully automate the scientific process — is one of the first artificial intelligence tools to go through the peer-review process at a leading academic journal . The resulting paper, published in Nature , updates a 2024 preprint that described the tool, including by toning down its reported capabilities. AI Scientist is a collection of ‘agents’ built on top of existing large language models, such as GPT-4o, that perform the full cycle of scientific discovery, from idea generation to testing its ideas to writing them up in a scientific paper.

items.title: ‘Grade inflation’ hits master’s and PhDs
items.snippet: The grades attained by master’s and PhD students at one large public university in the United States have crept up over the past two decades, without a demonstrable improvement in the quality of students’ work in that period. The spike likely amounts to what researchers call ‘grade inflation’ , a phenomenon that has scarcely been documented at graduate level. The reasons behind such inflation aren’t clear, but university professors might be incentivized to give higher grades to receive better student evaluations or to boost enrolment, says psychologist and study co-author Vivien Lee.

items.title: Environmental scientists must refocus
items.snippet: With science funding dwindling in the United States, environmental scientists must find new and imaginative ways to continue their research , argues socio-ecologist Michael Paul Nelson. Scientists should take inspiration from humanities researchers and pursue projects that re-use existing data, or collaborate with other teams to pool resources when financial backing is scarce. “If we don’t find fresh ways of keeping science moving forwards, those bearing the cost will be the most vulnerable members of our community: graduate students, postdocs and early-career scholars, cast aside from languishing projects,” Nelson writes.

items.title: To the rescue of rockhoppers
items.snippet: In the Falkland Islands, effects of climate change such as changing weather patterns and warming oceans are threatening the population of southern rockhopper penguins ( Eudyptes chrysocome ). To help the birds adapt, researchers on the islands are finding innovative ways to protect the penguin population , including efforts to artificially bolster their nests, and reintroduce co-operative species such as imperial cormorants ( Leucocarbo atriceps ). These scientists might not be able to alter ocean conditions, “but to help them on land, at the breeding sites, is something we can actually do”, says seabird biologist Sarah Crofts.

summary.overview: 이번 이슈는 고대 개 유전자, 뇌 활동 맵, AI 과학자 시스템, 학위 인플레이션, 환경 과학자 재정비, 기후 변화 대응 등 다양한 과학 주제를 다룹니다.
covered_item_titles: 개가 초기 인류의 가장 친한 친구였음을 밝혀낸다
뇌의 지역 간 소통 패턴을 매핑한 뇌 지도
피어 리뷰를 통한 AI 과학자 시스템
환경 과학자들의 연구 방향 재정비 필요
기후 변화에 대응하는 화산개 보호 노력

article_briefings.title: 개가 초기 인류의 가장 친한 친구였음을 밝혀낸다
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 연구자들이 유럽 전역의 고대 유해(14,000~16,000년 전)에서 최초로 개 유전자를 분석해 유전자 기록을 5,000년 전으로 되돌렸다.

article_briefings.title: 뇌의 지역 간 소통 패턴을 매핑한 뇌 지도
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 3,600명의 뇌 스캔 데이터를 기반으로 인생 전반의 뇌 지역 간 소통 패턴을 시각화한 첫 번째 뇌 지도가 발표되었다.

article_briefings.title: 피어 리뷰를 통한 AI 과학자 시스템
article_briefings.briefing_ko: AI 과학자 시스템이 주요 학술지 피어 리뷰를 통과하며, 과학적 과정 자동화의 새로운 가능성을 제시했다.

article_briefings.title: 석사·박사 학위의 등급 인플레이션 현상
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 미국 대학에서 석사·박사 학위 등급이 20년간 상승했으나, 학생들의 실질적 역량 향상은 확인되지 않았다.

article_briefings.title: 환경 과학자들의 연구 방향 재정비 필요
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 미국의 과학 기금 감소에 따라 환경 과학자들이 인문학 연구자로부터 창의적 접근 방식을 배우고 연구 방향을 재정비해야 한다.

article_briefings.title: 기후 변화에 대응하는 화산개 보호 노력
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 기후 변화로 인한 서남극 개 개체 수 감소를 막기 위해 연구자들이 기후 적응 전략을 수립하고 현지 보호 활동을 전개 중이다.
